patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/2] kernel/entry: add CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH to compile SUD out

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/2] kernel/entry: add CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH to compile SUD out

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/2] kernel/entry: add kernel.syscall_user_dispatch sysctl
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds a sysctl to control whether userspace can arm Syscall User Dispatch (SUD) via prctl or ptrace. The sysctl, kernel.syscall_user_dispatch, defaults to permitting SUD and is only present when the kernel is built with CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH. The change log provides documentation for this new sysctl.

review_comments.author: Thomas Gleixner
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns that an application with SUD enabled prior to the sysctl toggle would suddenly fail in operation if it requires to move the dispatch window. Also suggested improving the change log to explain the reasoning behind the design choice.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sat, Jun 27 2026 at 16:55, Gregory Price wrote:

Same comment vs. subject prefix.

> Add a matching sysctl to go with CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH.
>
> kernel.syscall_user_dispatch (default 1) controls whether userspace
> may arm SUD (both via prctl and ptrace).

What's SUD again? This is a changelog and not twatter.

> +	/* Arming can be denied at runtime via sysctl, disarming is allowed */
> +	if (mode != PR_SYS_DISPATCH_OFF && !syscall_user_dispatch_allowed)
> +		return -EPERM;

That might be similar to other sysctls, but if an application had it
enabled prior to the sysctl=off toggle, then that application will
suddenly fail in operation if it requires to move the dispatch window.

Thanks,

        tglx

review_comments.author: Thomas Gleixner
review_comments.summary: Reiterated the need for a clear explanation of the design choice in the change log, and suggested that a hard disable would result in undefined behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Jul 03 2026 at 13:26, Gregory Price wrote:
>> > +	/* Arming can be denied at runtime via sysctl, disarming is allowed */
>> > +	if (mode != PR_SYS_DISPATCH_OFF && !syscall_user_dispatch_allowed)
>> > +		return -EPERM;
>> 
>> That might be similar to other sysctls, but if an application had it
>> enabled prior to the sysctl=off toggle, then that application will
>> suddenly fail in operation if it requires to move the dispatch window.
>>
>
> If the admin is turning it off globally, is that not exactly what the
> admin wants?  The alternative is a hard disable that will simply result
> in undefined behavior (windows syscall ABI being interpreted at a linux
> syscall) instead of failing gracefully on the re-arm.
>
> This seemed like the better option.

Fair enough, but please explain such reasoning properly in the change
log.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Responded to Thomas Gleixner's concerns by explaining that a soft disable would allow the application to fail gracefully, whereas a hard disable would result in undefined behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
> > +	/* Arming can be denied at runtime via sysctl, disarming is allowed */
> > +	if (mode != PR_SYS_DISPATCH_OFF && !syscall_user_dispatch_allowed)
> > +		return -EPERM;
> 
> That might be similar to other sysctls, but if an application had it
> enabled prior to the sysctl=off toggle, then that application will
> suddenly fail in operation if it requires to move the dispatch window.
>

If the admin is turning it off globally, is that not exactly what the
admin wants?  The alternative is a hard disable that will simply result
in undefined behavior (windows syscall ABI being interpreted at a linux
syscall) instead of failing gracefully on the re-arm.

This seemed like the better option.

~Gregory

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new configuration option, CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH, to make Syscall User Dispatch (SUD) optional in the Linux kernel. SUD allows system calls to be redirected to userspace handlers and is used by native foreign-binary emulators like Wine/Proton. The patch introduces a Kconfig option for this feature, making it possible to disable it on systems where minimal or high-security configurations are desired. The change involves adding new code in several kernel files to support the configuration option and its dependencies.

review_comments.author: Thomas Gleixner
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer pointed out that the patch series lacks a cover letter and does not follow kernel documentation guidelines for subject prefixes and acronym usage. He suggested rephrasing the CONFIG option name in Kconfig to be more concise.

Reviewer Thomas Gleixner questioned the necessity of making Syscall User Dispatch optional for high security systems, arguing that enabling it requires additional steps (PRCTL and LD_PRELOAD) which may not be sufficient to ensure security.

The reviewer, Thomas Gleixner, questioned the terminology 'foreign-binary emulators' used in the patch description, pointing out that it could be misinterpreted as referring to different countries or universes, and clarified that SUD is not limited to emulators but can also intercept system calls within a specific IP address range.

Reviewer Thomas Gleixner noted that the function `syscall_user_dispatch_clear_on_dispatch()` should be marked as __always_inline to prevent aggressive compilers from inlining it out of line, potentially causing performance issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
Gregory!

On Sat, Jun 27 2026 at 16:55, Gregory Price wrote:

First a few formal notes.

    - Multi patch series require a cover letter

    - The subject prefix is not a matter of personal preference

See https://docs.kernel.org/process/maintainer-tip.html and the related
generic process documentation.

Please also refrain from made up acronyms in the subject line. What the
heck is SUD and why needs this to spell out the CONFIG option name
prominently.

    syscall_user_dispatch: Make it configurable in Kconfig

or something like that is concise and clear, no?

---

I buy the miminal system aspect, but high security is just a voodoo
argument. Why?

  1) The functionality needs to be enabled with a PRCTL, which can be
     filtered.

  2) It requires LD_PRELOAD to be effective

If your high security system allows #2 then it's not a high security
system to begin with. If you fail to add the proper filters then it does
not pass the test either.

I agree that disabling it alltogether reduces the effort, but it's not a
prerequisite.

---

Huch?

What is foreign? Different country, different universe or different
mindset?

It's also not restricted to emulators. It allows to intercept and abort
system calls which are issued within a certain IP address range and
redirect them to a emulator or debugger.

---

Wants to be __always_inline as otherwise agressive compilers like CLANG
happily put it out of line.

Thanks,

        tglx

review_comments.author: Thomas Gleixner
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Thomas Gleixner noted that the patch introduces a new Kconfig option SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH without properly checking if it's enabled before using its functionality, and requested that the check be added to ensure correct behavior.

The reviewer noted that an application enabling SUD prior to disabling it via sysctl would suddenly fail due to the changed dispatch window, and requested consideration for this edge case.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sat, Jun 27 2026 at 16:55, Gregory Price wrote:

Same comment vs. subject prefix.

---

That might be similar to other sysctls, but if an application had it
enabled prior to the sysctl=off toggle, then that application will
suddenly fail in operation if it requires to move the dispatch window.

Thanks,

        tglx

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author responded to concerns about the security implications of Syscall User Dispatch, arguing it can be used for malicious purposes beyond traditional sandbox evasion or privilege escalation, such as stealing CPU time from a compromised task without requiring LD_PRELOAD or context switching. The author claims this is a powerful tool for writing implants and wants to make it optional.
review_comments.raw_body:
sure, it can be filtered, but not all software runs in a sandbox and not
all attack vectors need end in priv-esc or launching new tasks.  It
does not require LD_PRELOAD to be useful to an attacker.

just as an example, if i land remote execution in a task, i can enable
syscall user dispatch and just steal cpu time from that task whenever it
makes a syscall without having to do any of the traditional tricks of
overwriting GDT entries to get hooks.  No need for ld preloading or even
leaving the active task's context.

syscall user dispatch is just a really clean, powerful tool for writing
implants.  I've been playing with it for the better part of 2 years and
just realized I don't want it enabled (or even present at all) on some
of my machines / machines I manage and there's no way to do that.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author responded to feedback that turning off SUD globally would be more desirable than a hard disable, explaining it allows for graceful failure instead of undefined behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
If the admin is turning it off globally, is that not exactly what the
admin wants?  The alternative is a hard disable that will simply result
in undefined behavior (windows syscall ABI being interpreted at a linux
syscall) instead of failing gracefully on the re-arm.

This seemed like the better option.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Thomas Gleixner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Thomas Gleixner requested that the patch description include a clear explanation of why CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH is being added and how it interacts with existing code.
review_comments.raw_body:
Fair enough, but please explain such reasoning properly in the change
log.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 2/2] kernel/entry: add kernel.syscall_user_dispatch sysctl
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: A sysctl is added to control the kernel's syscall dispatch behavior.
